summary: DHH argues that readers of technical books, especially for emerging open source technologies, don't care about perfect polish — they care about getting useful information quickly. He uses the success of the 'Agile Web Development with Rails' beta book (5,000+ orders) as proof that releasing imperfect documentation early beats waiting for perfection. The alternative for readers is assembling fragmented knowledge from wikis, APIs, mailing lists, and IRC logs themselves. He advocates for publishers to experiment with new formats, quality levels, and mini-books for niche topics.
key_points: For emerging open source technologies, readers value speed of access over polish — the 'last 20%' of perfection matters only to publishers
Open source documentation culture prioritizes code over docs, creating fragmented knowledge spread across wikis, APIs, blogs, and IRC
The beta book model proved commercially successful, with 5,000+ orders for an unfinished Rails book
There's an untapped market for mini-books and varied documentation formats beyond the traditional comprehensive reference
Publishing has never been cheaper to experiment with, and early results suggest readers will reward it
money_quotes: How much do you think people care about an off comma or page break if the alternative is doing all the work themselves?
Only the publishers and their caring for the craft of creating beautiful books. We, the readers, just want the information faster, so we can get up to speed with less pain.
It still belongs to a culture that places code highest and which often leaves documentation to linger or be incomplete.
To get a sane narrative, you have to dig a little. Collect your own mosaic.
I want to consume better documentation in a variety of forms and quality levels.
It's never been cheaper to do so or more likely to succeed.
key_insight: Perfectionism in technical publishing is a publisher's concern, not a reader's — for emerging technologies, shipping useful-but-imperfect documentation fast beats waiting for polish.
